title: Finite-size effects in amorphous thin Co$_{70}$Zr$_{30}$ layers
authors: Vladislav Kurichenko, Parul Rani, Björgvin Hjörvarsson
abstract: Profound finite size effects are observed in both the moment and ordering temperature in thin Co$_{70}$Zr$_{30}$ layers. The results are consistent with the presence of interface regions with reduced magnetic interactions and moment. The extension of this region is determined to be around 1 nm thick at each interface. Above and near the apparent critical temperature, the magnetic properties can be understood in terms of Griffith phases.
summary: This paper examines how the thickness of amorphous Co70Zr30 thin films affects their magnetic properties. As the films get thinner, the magnetic ordering temperature and saturation magnetization decrease due to the influence of interfacial regions. The study identifies a scaling relationship between these properties and the inverse of the thickness. Additionally, the researchers observe the presence of a Griffiths phase in very thin layers, where magnetic clusters persist above the critical temperature.
main_problem: The study investigates how finite-size effects in amorphous Co70Zr30 thin layers influence magnetic properties, specifically the reduction in magnetic moment and ordering temperature.
main_result: The researchers found that decreasing layer thickness leads to a reduction in Tc and Ms following a 1/L scaling law, driven by interfacial regions with reduced magnetic interactions, and observed Griffiths phase behavior in the thinnest films.
method: The study uses dc magnetron sputtering for film deposition, GIXRD and XRR for structural characterization, and SQUID magnetometry and MOKE for magnetic measurements.
model_or_system: The system consists of amorphous Co70Zr30 single-layer thin films with thicknesses ranging from 25 to 400 Angstroms, modeled as an interior magnetic region flanked by two interfacial regions.
key_observables: Saturation magnetization (Ms), magnetic ordering temperature (Tc), coercivity, interatomic distances, and magnetic anisotropy.
important_parameters: Thickness range (25-400 A), interface thickness (approx 10 A), and the transition from ferromagnetic to Griffiths phase behavior.
main_assumptions: The magnetic layer is modeled as having an interior region and two interfacial regions that are effectively 'magnetically dead'.
figures_summary: Fig 1 shows GIXRD patterns; Fig 2 shows XRR patterns; Fig 3 shows thickness-dependent MOKE hysteresis loops; Fig 4 shows temperature-dependent magnetization (SQUID) and Tc reduction.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of finite-size effects, describes the experimental deposition and structural characterization, presents magnetic measurement results, applies a scaling model to analyze thickness dependence, and discusses the emergence of the Griffiths phase.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

FIG. 4. M(T) dependence of Co70Zr30 layers with diﬀerent thicknesses, determined using SQUID. Inset shows plot of the data in reduced units.
FIG. 2. XRR patterns of Co70Zr30 with diﬀerent thickness. The solid red lines show the GenX [16] ﬁts of the reﬂectivity data. The patterns were oﬀset for clarity.

Figure 3 shows the M(H) dependencies measured at 100 K for three of the samples using MOKE. Data for the 25 Å-thick ﬁlm is not shown, as its magnetic order- ing temperature lies well below 100 K. As seen in the ﬁg- ure, both the coercivity and the saturation moment are aﬀected by the thickness of the layers. When growing random amorphous alloys in a ﬁeld a well deﬁned uni- axial anisotropy can develop, if the growth temperature is below the magnetic ordering temperature [17]. The origin of the anisotropy is believed to be the interplay between the external ﬁeld and the evolving moment, af- fecting the atomic conﬁgurations in the growing sample.
